title: Mass generation at a fixed point: A Functional Renormalization Group Study of the tricritical O($N$) model in $d=3$ and $N=\infty$
abstract: Renormalization group (RG) fixed points are commonly associated with scale invariance and a divergent correlation length. We show that this connection can fail in the tricritical $O(N)$ model in three dimensions in the limit $N\to\infty$. Revisiting the line of fixed points identified by Bardeen, Moshe, and Bander, we use the functional renormalization group to clarify the mechanism leading to mass generation at its singular endpoint (the BMB fixed point). We demonstrate that the generated mass is nonuniversal and originates from the nonanalytic structure of the effective potential. We show that the critical exponent $ν$ which takes the value $ν= 1/2$ along the regular part of the BMB line, that is, for $0 \leq λ< λ_{\rm BMB}$, jumps to $ν= 1/3$ on the singular part of this line with the BMB FP, corresponding to $λ= λ_{\rm BMB}$, being the pivotal point between these two regimes. We also show how its singular potential emerges dynamically along the renormalization flow.
summary: This theoretical paper uses Functional Renormalization Group methods to study mass generation in the tricritical O(N) model. It demonstrates that at a specific fixed point, the generated mass is nonuniversal and depends on UV initial conditions. Crucially, it reveals a discontinuity in the critical exponent as the system crosses into this singular fixed point regime.
main_problem: The study investigates mass generation at a fixed point (FP) in the tricritical O(N) model, specifically addressing how this occurs when standard connections between FPs and scale invariance break down.
main_result: The generated mass is shown to be nonuniversal, originating from the nonanalytic structure of the effective potential. Furthermore, the critical exponent $\nu$ exhibits a discontinuous jump at the singular BMB fixed point ($\nu=1/2 \to 1/3$).
method: The analysis heavily relies on the Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) approach, utilizing Wilsonian flow equations and approximations like the Local Potential Approximation (LPA).
model_or_system: The model is the tricritical O(N) sigma model in three dimensions ($d=3$) analyzed in the large-$N$ limit ($N=\infty$). The system exhibits a line of fixed points parameterized by $\lambda$, terminating at the singular BMB FP.
key_observables: Critical exponent $\nu$, effective potential $U_{eff}(\phi)$, and the dimensionful physical mass $M_0$.
important_parameters: The coupling constant parameter $\lambda$ (or $\tau$), the dimensionality $d=3$, and the large-$N$ limit ($N=\infty$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Local Potential Approximation (LPA) for solving the exact flow equation, and in some parts, assumes certain terms become negligible in the $N \to \infty$ limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate potential parameterizations ($\bar{V}$ vs $\bar{ho}$) showing discontinuities at the BMB FP, and track the evolution of potentials and mass squared over RG time to demonstrate non-universal flow.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the FRG framework for the model, then analyzing the regular line of FPs (BMB line), constructing the singular counterpart, and finally using perturbation theory around the BMB FP to derive the mechanism for mass generation and the jump in critical exponents.
why_interesting: While focused on statistical mechanics, the use of advanced non-perturbative RG techniques (FRG) to study emergent phenomena like dynamical mass generation is highly relevant to understanding quantum field theories underlying many condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
